narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and LSAT score of 26. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on students' academic and background details, estimated an 78% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was in the bottom 10% (a 1 out of 10), far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position around the 5th or 6th decile; this low performance was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining strong grades through law school. Your undergraduate GPA played the second-largest role, and in a positive way—its solid level near the average of 3.21 helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing, showing you have a foundation of academic capability. Coming in third was your LSAT score of 26, which is notably below the dataset average of about 37 and contributed by nudging the overall outlook toward failure, reflecting concerns about standardized test readiness for the rigors ahead. Compared to typical students in the dataset, who often have stronger mid-to-high decile rankings in later school years and higher LSATs, your combination painted a riskier picture despite your full-time commitment and decent undergrad record. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from an upper-middle-income family pursuing law—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements—but the model weighs these patterns heavily from past bar outcomes. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retaking the LSAT to align closer to averages, as even modest gains there could flip future predictions. Consider preparatory programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself to build those key strengths. Retake the LSAT soon, aiming for at least the mid-30s, and seek feedback from advisors on bolstering late-stage school performance. With these steps, you can reapply stronger next cycle and show the model—and admissions teams—your true bar-passing potential.
